straight_beat.name: Don Pellegrino
straight_beat.column:
The Mets lost again, and the ledger keeps getting heavier. Another loss to the A’s, another 11-6 drubbing, and the record now reads 7-8. That’s the Flushing ledger.  

You could argue the A’s were a team built to beat the Mets — a squad that’s been a thorn in our side for years, always finding ways to outslug us, outpitch us, and outthink us. But this wasn’t just about the A’s. It was about the Mets’ inability to string together a decent series. Again. The same old story: starters can’t hold leads, the bullpen can’t close, and the offense can’t capitalize when it matters.  

Let’s start with the pitching. The starters, for all their talent, keep falling apart. Last night’s game was a case study. The rotation, which should be the backbone of this team, looked like a group of guys trying to remember the playbook. The A’s didn’t just beat us — they made us look like a team that’s still figuring out how to pitch in the modern era. And when the starters can’t get through

optimist.name: Sheila 'Sheesh' Fortunato
optimist.column:
The Mets lost yesterday, sure. But let me tell you something—this team ain’t done yet. You see that? They’re still in the game, still breathing, still got that fire in their bellies. Yeah, the A’s handed them a 11-6 drubbing, but you know what? That’s just the universe giving ‘em a little shake, like a pizza dough—flattening it out, ready to rise again.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: “Sheesh, the Mets are 7-8, two games behind. How’s that optimism?” Well, let me ask you this: How many times have we seen a team come back from a 2.0-game deficit? How many times have we seen a team like this—full of young talent, full of heart—turn that deficit into a drive? You think the front office’s got their eyes on the prize?

pessimist.name: Sal Marusiak
pessimist.column:
The Mets’ 11-6 loss to the A’s wasn’t just another game; it was a masterclass in how this team turns potential into panic. You’d think a seven-game lead in the standings would feel like a safety net, but no—this franchise has always been allergic to comfort. The A’s didn’t just beat them; they embarrassed them, dismantling a lineup that looked momentarily competent before crumbling under the weight of its own incompetence. Nine runs in the first inning? That’s not a lead; that’s a warning shot. And yet, here we are, two games behind in the division, because someone somewhere decided “grit” is a viable strategy instead of, you know, actually pitching.  

The bullpen, that cursed appendage of the Mets organization, once again proved why it’s a liability. You can’t blame the starters for giving up 11 runs—hell, they’re paid to keep the game close until the eighth inning. But when the relievers come in, they don’t just fail; they *cele
